refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output to support evaluation against customer-defined energy efficiency targets.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the capability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a specific target value. The refined FR focuses on the functional requirement—providing visibility and traceability of energy performance—which is solution-neutral and within system control. Achievement of the target is a verification criterion (acceptance), not a functional requirement. The refined FR is atomic: it describes a single, measurable capability without 'and'/'or' conjunctions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the system can continuously or periodically measure energy_used (kWh or equivalent) and work_output (tons moved or equivalent task units), compute energy_per_ton, and expose this metric via operator interface, logs, or telemetry such that it can be compared against E_target. Verify measurement accuracy and completeness over the agreed evaluation period.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: Measure and log total energy consumed by the NGHE solution during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: Measure and log total work output (e.g., mass moved, distance traveled, or task-specific units) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: Calculate energy efficiency metric (energy per unit work output) from logged measurements.
FR-NGHE-003.4: Report energy efficiency metric to authorized stakeholders (operator, site manager, maintenance) in real-time or near-real-time.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument work output (tonnage moved) and energy input (power consumed) at independent measurement points; aggregate and compute ratio (energy/ton) at a centralized analytics layer; compare against target threshold and generate compliance reports.

DP2: Deploy real-time energy metering on the power supply circuit and work-output sensors on the load/displacement mechanism; stream both signals to a local edge controller that calculates and logs energy_per_ton continuously; trigger alerts if threshold is exceeded.

DP3: Capture energy consumption via utility billing data (kWh invoices) and correlate with independently logged work output (ton-moved records) over the evaluation period; compute aggregate energy_per_ton ratio post-hoc and validate against target.

DP4: Instrument energy input at the source (power meter) and work output at the point of load transfer (load cell or displacement sensor); implement dual independent data streams with time-synchronized logging; compute energy_per_ton on-demand via a decoupled calculation service that accepts both streams as inputs.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4: Instrument energy input at the source (power meter) and work output at the point of load transfer (load cell or displacement sensor); implement dual independent data streams with time-synchronized logging; compute energy_per_ton on-demand via a decoupled calculation service that accepts both streams as inputs.

potential_sources: Time synchronization drift between energy and work-output sensors could introduce systematic error in ratio calculation; mitigation: enforce synchronized clock source (NTP or hardware trigger).

Sensor failure modes (e.g., load cell drift, power meter saturation) could corrupt both streams simultaneously if not independently validated; mitigation: implement per-stream health checks and anomaly detection.

Calculation service dependency on both data streams creates a single point of failure for reporting; mitigation: design calculation service as stateless and idempotent, allow replay from logged streams.

Definition of 'work output' (ton-moved) may be ambiguous if multiple load types or partial loads occur; mitigation: establish clear operational definition and calibration protocol in acceptance criteria.
coupling_check.notes: DP4 achieves low coupling by separating measurement (energy and work) from calculation and reporting. Each measurement stream is independent; the calculation service is a thin, stateless aggregator. This allows independent evolution of sensor technology, data logging, and analytics without redesign. DP1 and DP3 are less favorable: DP1 lacks specificity on measurement independence; DP3 introduces latency and relies on external billing data (low control). DP2 couples real-time calculation to edge hardware, reducing flexibility for post-hoc analysis or algorithm refinement.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Energy consumption is measurable at a single point (power supply inlet or main circuit breaker). RISK: If the system has multiple independent power sources or significant parasitic loads outside the measurement boundary, the ratio will be inaccurate. MITIGATION: Define measurement boundary explicitly in acceptance criteria; validate via energy balance audit.

ASSUMPTION: Work output (tonnage moved) is directly observable and unambiguous. RISK: Partial loads, multiple load types, or indirect work (e.g., idle time, setup) may not be captured consistently. MITIGATION: Establish operational definition and calibration protocol; log work-output metadata (load type, distance, conditions).

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough to average transient effects and sensor noise. RISK: Short evaluation windows or high variability in operating conditions could yield unreliable ratios. MITIGATION: Specify minimum evaluation period and acceptable variance bounds in acceptance criteria.

ASSUMPTION: Time synchronization between energy and work-output sensors is achievable and maintained. RISK: Clock drift or network latency could introduce systematic bias in pairing energy and work events. MITIGATION: Implement hardware-level synchronization (e.g., GPS, PTP) or post-hoc alignment with sufficient logging granularity.

ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined energy efficiency target (E_target) is stable and measurable in the same units (kWh/ton or equivalent). RISK: Target may be ambiguous, context-dependent, or subject to change mid-evaluation. MITIGATION: Formalize target definition in contract; include tolerance band and revision protocol.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies do not conflict with instrumentation or data logging. RISK: Safety constraints (e.g., no additional wiring, restricted sensor placement) could limit measurement accuracy. MITIGATION: Coordinate with safety team early; validate sensor placement and installation method against safety requirements.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Measurement boundary for energy input (e.g., 'main power inlet to the work system'); (2) Definition of work output (e.g., 'tonnage of material moved from point A to point B, excluding idle and setup time'); (3) Evaluation period and acceptable variance (e.g., 'over 30 consecutive operating days, with energy_per_ton computed as rolling 8-hour average'); (4) Synchronization tolerance (e.g., 'energy and work-output events paired within ±5 seconds'); (5) Acceptance criteria for sensor health and data quality (e.g., 'no more than 5% of data points flagged as anomalous'). This will reduce ambiguity in DP realization and IM packaging, and enable clear traceability from FR to acceptance test.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized energy measurement, data stream management, and on-demand energy efficiency calculation service that orchestrates dual independent sensor inputs (power meter and load cell/displacement sensor) with time-synchronized logging and decoupled computation of energy_per_ton metric.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmit raw sensor streams (power input, work output) and computed energy_per_ton metrics to monitoring/analytics systems

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receive requests to compute energy_per_ton on-demand and configure sensor logging parameters

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emit alerts if energy efficiency metrics fall outside acceptable bounds or sensor synchronization fails

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicate with Vehicle-mounted sensors (power meter, load cell) and receive time-synchronized data streams

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: NGHE Solution is the logical home for energy measurement, data orchestration, and efficiency calculation. Dual sensor streams, time-synchronized logging, and decoupled calculation service are all core to NGHE's mission. Minor diffusion: sensor hardware itself resides on Vehicle; NGHE owns the measurement logic and computation.

Poor fit: Vehicle owns the physical sensors (power meter, load cell) but not the data aggregation, synchronization, or calculation logic. Placing DP here would scatter energy computation across vehicle firmware and create tight coupling to vehicle-specific hardware. Calculation service would diffuse to external systems.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation is a control/command interface, not a data processing or measurement system. Energy measurement and efficiency calculation are orthogonal to teleoperation concerns. High diffusion: would require Teleoperation to manage sensor streams and computation, violating separation of concerns.

Poor fit: Safety Services consumes energy metrics (e.g., to detect anomalies) but should not own the measurement and calculation logic. Placing DP here conflates safety monitoring with energy instrumentation. Diffusion: raw sensor streams and calculation service belong in a dedicated measurement module.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of energy measurement and efficiency calculation because: (1) it is the system responsible for optimizing energy performance; (2) dual independent sensor streams and time-synchronized logging are core to NGHE's data integrity requirements; (3) the decoupled calculation service is a reusable, domain-specific component that belongs in the energy management layer, not scattered across vehicle or safety subsystems; (4) external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) align with NGHE's role as a measurement and analytics provider.
containment_risks: Sensor hardware (power meter, load cell) resides on Vehicle; NGHE must establish a clear Network Link interface to receive raw sensor data without owning the sensors themselves. Risk: if sensor calibration or hardware faults occur, responsibility boundaries must be explicit.

Time synchronization between dual streams requires either Vehicle-side timestamping or NGHE-side clock correction. If Vehicle provides unsynchronized data, NGHE must implement synchronization logic, increasing complexity. Risk: clock drift or network latency could degrade synchronization quality.

Decoupled calculation service may be invoked by multiple consumers (Teleoperation, Safety Services, external analytics). Risk: if demand patterns are unpredictable, the service could become a bottleneck. Mitigation: design as a stateless, scalable microservice.

Energy_per_ton metric depends on both power and work measurements. If either sensor fails or drifts, the metric becomes unreliable. Risk: NGHE must implement sensor health monitoring and alert SafetyAlert if data quality degrades.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 directly and completely addresses the FR. The dual independent sensor streams (power meter at source, load cell/displacement sensor at load transfer point) with time-synchronized logging provide the measurement foundation. The decoupled calculation service that computes energy_per_ton on-demand from both streams is the logical mechanism to satisfy the requirement of 'measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output.' The DP is solution-neutral (does not over-specify implementation details like sensor brands or communication protocols) and is verifiable against the acceptance criterion: energy_per_ton ≤ E_target.

diffusion_risks: Sensor hardware ownership boundary: Power meter and load cell reside on Vehicle; NGHE owns measurement logic and calculation. Risk of unclear responsibility if sensor calibration drift or hardware fault occurs—must establish explicit handoff protocol via Network Link interface.

Time synchronization logic placement: If Vehicle provides unsynchronized raw timestamps, NGHE must implement clock correction. If Vehicle provides pre-synchronized data, NGHE receives clean streams. Current DP does not specify which party owns synchronization—recommend explicit clarification in interface contract to avoid diffusion of synchronization responsibility.

Calculation service scalability and invocation patterns: Decoupled calculation service may be called by Teleoperation (real-time efficiency feedback), Safety Services (anomaly detection), and external analytics. Risk: if demand is bursty or unpredictable, service becomes a bottleneck. Mitigation: design as stateless microservice with async queuing; document expected throughput and latency SLAs.

Sensor health monitoring and alert routing: DP requires 'time-synchronized logging' but does not explicitly state who monitors sensor health (e.g., power meter drift, load cell signal loss). If NGHE detects degraded data quality, it must emit SafetyAlert. Risk: if sensor fault detection logic is incomplete, unreliable energy_per_ton metrics could be reported without warning. Recommend explicit sensor health check as part of calculation service pre-flight.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution scores 0.92 containment / 0.08 diffusion—excellent fit. The module boundary is well-defined: NGHE owns data orchestration, time-synchronized logging, and the decoupled calculation service. Vehicle owns physical sensors and raw data acquisition; NGHE consumes via Network Link. Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces are outbound, appropriate for a measurement/analytics provider. Tasking interface (inbound) allows external systems to request on-demand computation and configure logging. The four external interfaces align cleanly with NGHE's role and do not leak core DP logic to other modules. Minor risk: ensure Network Link interface contract specifies data format, timestamp precision, and synchronization guarantees to prevent ambiguity about which party corrects clock skew.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion 'energy_per_ton ≤ E_target over agreed evaluation period' is directly measurable from NGHE's output. NGHE computes energy_per_ton on-demand; external systems (e.g., analytics, Safety Services) can log and aggregate these values over the evaluation period and compare against E_target. Verification requires: (1) access to raw sensor streams (power, work output) to audit calculation correctness, (2) confirmation that time-synchronized logging is accurate (e.g., via timestamp audit), (3) confirmation that sensor calibration is traceable to worksite standards. All three are feasible if NGHE exposes Telemetry interface with sufficient detail.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR is allocated to NGHE Solution as the primary owner. Responsibility boundaries are explicit: NGHE measures and computes; Vehicle provides sensors; Safety Services consumes alerts; Teleoperation and external analytics consume energy_per_ton metrics. Worksite safety plan and operational policies are external constraints that inform E_target and evaluation period—these are inputs to the system, not owned by NGHE. Allocation is unambiguous and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Clarify time-synchronization ownership in Network Link interface contract: specify whether Vehicle provides pre-synchronized timestamps or raw timestamps that NGHE must correct. If NGHE corrects, document clock correction algorithm and acceptable drift tolerance.

Add explicit sensor health monitoring to DP: 'Implement continuous health checks on power meter and load cell (e.g., signal range validation, rate-of-change anomaly detection). If sensor health degrades below acceptable threshold, emit SafetyAlert and flag energy_per_ton metric as unreliable until sensor is restored.'

Document calculation service SLA: specify expected latency for on-demand energy_per_ton computation, throughput (requests/sec), and behavior under overload (queue, reject, or degrade gracefully). This mitigates bottleneck risk.

Define Telemetry interface schema: specify which raw sensor streams and computed metrics are transmitted, at what frequency, and with what precision. This enables external systems to audit energy_per_ton calculation and supports verification against E_target.

Establish sensor calibration and traceability protocol: specify how power meter and load cell calibration is verified, documented, and maintained. NGHE should log calibration metadata (date, standard, uncertainty) alongside energy_per_ton metrics to support acceptance criterion verification.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. However, the gate results expose four critical diffusion risks and five recommended revisions that are safety-relevant and architectural in nature. Rather than stopping at this level, decomposition must continue to the next level to explicitly address: (1) time-synchronization ownership and clock correction logic, (2) sensor health monitoring and alert routing, (3) calculation service SLA and scalability, and (4) Telemetry interface schema and sensor calibration traceability. These are not minor implementation details—they are functional requirements in their own right that must be allocated to specific modules and verified. High safety criticality demands that sensor health, data quality assurance, and alert propagation are not left implicit. Decomposing FR4 into sub-FRs will force explicit ownership, interface contracts, and verification criteria for these concerns.
next_level_sub_frs: FR4.1: Ensure time-synchronized acquisition and logging of power meter and load cell data streams with documented clock correction and acceptable drift tolerance.

FR4.2: Monitor sensor health (power meter and load cell) continuously and emit SafetyAlert if signal quality degrades below acceptable threshold; flag energy_per_ton metrics as unreliable until sensor is restored.
FR4.3: Compute energy_per_ton on-demand from synchronized power and work output streams with documented calculation algorithm, latency SLA, and graceful behavior under overload.

FR4.4: Transmit raw sensor streams and computed energy_per_ton metrics via Telemetry interface with specified schema, frequency, precision, and sensor calibration metadata to enable external audit and verification against E_target.

revise.why: The current DP4 is logically sound but under-specifies critical concerns flagged by the gate review. Revise DP4 to explicitly incorporate: (1) time-synchronization strategy (pre-synchronized vs. raw timestamps with NGHE-side correction), (2) sensor health monitoring algorithm and alert routing, (3) calculation service invocation pattern and SLA, (4) Telemetry schema and calibration metadata logging. These revisions will make DP4 more complete and will serve as the basis for deriving the four sub-FRs above. The revised DP4 should remain solution-neutral but must be more explicit about the logical mechanisms for data quality assurance and interface contracts.

assumptions: Vehicle provides either pre-synchronized timestamps or raw timestamps with sufficient precision for NGHE to correct clock skew within acceptable tolerance (assumed ≤ 100 ms for energy_per_ton accuracy).
Power meter and load cell are calibrated to worksite standards and calibration metadata is available to NGHE for logging and audit.
Calculation service demand is predictable or can be handled by async queuing without violating latency SLA for real-time feedback use cases (e.g., Teleoperation).
SafetyAlert routing is established and Safety Services will act on sensor health degradation alerts within acceptable response time.
External systems (analytics, Safety Services, Teleoperation) have access to Telemetry interface and can audit energy_per_ton calculation against raw sensor streams.
open_risks: Time-synchronization ownership ambiguity: if Vehicle provides unsynchronized raw timestamps, NGHE must implement clock correction. If correction algorithm is incorrect or drift tolerance is not met, energy_per_ton metrics will be inaccurate. Mitigation: explicit interface contract specifying synchronization guarantees and correction algorithm.

Sensor health monitoring incompleteness: if sensor fault detection logic does not cover all failure modes (e.g., power meter drift, load cell signal loss, intermittent connection), unreliable energy_per_ton metrics could be reported without warning. Mitigation: comprehensive sensor health check specification and continuous validation.

Calculation service bottleneck: if demand is bursty or unpredictable, service may become a bottleneck for real-time feedback (Teleoperation) or safety-critical anomaly detection (Safety Services). Mitigation: design as stateless microservice with async queuing and document SLA.

Telemetry interface schema ambiguity: if Telemetry interface does not transmit sufficient detail (raw sensor streams, calibration metadata, computation timestamps), external systems cannot audit energy_per_ton calculation or verify against E_target. Mitigation: explicit schema specification and precision requirements.

Sensor calibration traceability: if calibration metadata is not logged alongside energy_per_ton metrics, verification against E_target may be challenged (e.g., 'was the power meter calibrated at the time of measurement?'). Mitigation: establish calibration protocol and metadata logging requirement.
evidence_needed: Interface contract for Network Link specifying data format, timestamp precision, synchronization guarantees, and clock correction algorithm (if applicable).
Sensor health monitoring specification: list of monitored parameters (e.g., signal range, rate-of-change, connection status), thresholds for degradation, and alert routing to Safety Services.
Calculation service SLA: latency (e.g., ≤ 500 ms for on-demand computation), throughput (e.g., ≥ 100 requests/sec), and behavior under overload (queue depth, rejection policy).

Telemetry interface schema: JSON/protobuf specification of transmitted fields (raw power, raw work output, computed energy_per_ton, timestamps, calibration metadata, sensor health status), transmission frequency, and precision (e.g., power in watts, work output in joules, energy_per_ton in kJ/ton).

Sensor calibration and traceability protocol: procedure for verifying calibration, documentation format, metadata to be logged (calibration date, standard, uncertainty, next calibration due date), and audit trail.
Clock correction algorithm specification (if NGHE is responsible): algorithm (e.g., NTP, PTP, linear drift correction), acceptable drift tolerance, and validation method.

Verification plan for acceptance criterion: procedure for aggregating energy_per_ton metrics over evaluation period, comparing against E_target, and auditing calculation correctness using raw sensor streams.
